title: Resolve Review P3: Fail safely on malformed odd-length WAV payloads
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
The bundled WAV decoder assumes the PCM payload length is always even. A malformed file with one trailing byte will crash the decoder instead of returning a normal format error.

## What The Code Does Today
- `decodePCM16WaveFile(_:)` walks the payload with `stride(..., by: 2)`.
- Each iteration slices `data[offset..<(offset + 2)]` without checking that two bytes remain.
- If the payload length is odd, the last slice goes out of bounds and traps.

## Why This Is A Problem
- The decoder is part of the whisper smoke path, and malformed input should fail safely.
- Crashing on a bad file is worse than returning the same unsupported-format error path the rest of the decoder already uses.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- The bundled sample or a locally supplied test WAV is truncated during copy and ends with one extra byte.
- `decodePCM16WaveFile(_:)` reaches the final offset and slices past `data.count`.
- Meetless crashes during decoding instead of reporting that the file format is unsupported.

## Evidence
**File:** `MeetlessPackages/WhisperCppBridge/Sources/WhisperCppBridge/WaveFileDecoder.swift`
**Line(s):** 14-30

```swift
func decodePCM16WaveFile(_ url: URL) throws -> [Float] {
    let data = try Data(contentsOf: url)
    ...
    return stride(from: 44, to: data.count, by: 2).map { offset in
        data[offset..<(offset + 2)].withUnsafeBytes { bytes in
            let sample = Int16(littleEndian: bytes.load(as: Int16.self))
            ...
        }
    }
}
```

**Why this is a problem:** malformed input reaches an unchecked out-of-bounds slice.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: validate even payload length before decoding
**Pros:** Simple and explicit.
**Cons:** Still assumes the same fixed header model.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — guard each iteration with `offset + 1 < data.count`
**Pros:** Prevents the trap even if earlier validation changes.
**Cons:** Slightly less direct than validating once up front.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Odd-length PCM payloads do not crash the decoder.
- [ ] Malformed WAV input returns a normal decoder error instead of trapping.
- [ ] A unit test covers at least one truncated odd-length payload.

title: Resolve Review P1: Exercise generated-notes rollback inside transaction
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
The existing generated-notes failure test throws before the transactional write path, so it does not prove rollback after partial file changes.

## What The Code Does Today
- The forced failure guard exits before the staging/replace/restore logic runs.

## Why This Is A Problem
- D8 requires failed generation to leave the session unchanged, including mid-write failures.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- Writing `generated-notes.json` succeeds, manifest replacement fails, and the bundle is left inconsistent.

## Evidence
File: `MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/SessionRepository.swift`
Line(s): 470, 502, 515
Snippet: `try replaceItem(at: notesURL, with: stagedNotesURL)`
Why this proves the issue: the rollback code exists but needs a test that fails inside this block.

## Proposed Fix
Recommended: add a test-only fault injection point inside the transaction, after notes replacement and before manifest replacement, then assert manifest bytes and notes absence/prior notes are preserved.
Tradeoff: small internal test seam.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A test fails inside the transaction after notes replacement.
- [ ] First-time failure leaves no notes file and unchanged manifest.
- [ ] Full macOS test suite passes.
close_reason: Fixed: tests now inject failure inside generated-notes transaction and verify first-time rollback leaves bundle unchanged.

title: Phase 3 / Story 1: Surface transcript snapshot persistence failures
description:
Phase 3 / Story 1. Honest transcript snapshot persistence failure handling. File scope: RecordingViewModel.swift, SessionRepository.swift, and saved-session honesty surfaces. Planning context: replace the silent transcript.json write path with visible degraded or incomplete persistence signaling so the operator can tell when the UI transcript moved ahead of the persisted snapshot.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Transcript snapshot write failures are visible in recording state and logs.
- A failed snapshot write leaves an honest degraded or incomplete marker in persisted metadata.
- Reviewers can inject a repository write failure and see bounded, user-visible handling.

## Verify
- Inject a transcript snapshot write failure during recording.
- Confirm the recording UI and logs surface the persistence problem without crashing the recording flow.
- Reopen the saved session and confirm its honesty markers reflect that the persisted transcript snapshot degraded or fell behind.
acceptance_criteria: Transcript snapshot write failures are visible in recording state and logs. A failed snapshot write leaves an honest degraded or incomplete marker in persisted metadata. Reviewers can inject a repository write failure and see bounded, user-visible handling.
close_reason: Completed: surfaced transcript snapshot persistence failures in live and saved session state

title: Resolve Review P2: Keep generated-notes reads inside bundle
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
Meetless reads the generated-notes filename from the manifest. A tampered manifest could point the read outside the saved-session bundle.

## What The Code Does Today
- `loadGeneratedNotes` appends `manifest.generatedNotesFilename` directly to the session directory.

## Why This Is A Problem
- Saved-session reads should stay inside the session bundle and not trust arbitrary manifest paths.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- Edit `generatedNotesFilename` to a traversal path that points to another local JSON file; opening detail attempts to read it.

## Evidence
File: `MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/SessionRepository.swift`
Line(s): 593, 601
Snippet: `session.directoryURL.appendingPathComponent(generatedNotesFilename, isDirectory: false)`
Why this proves the issue: manifest-provided filename is trusted without basename or containment validation.

## Proposed Fix
Recommended: ignore manifest-provided notes filenames in v1 and use the constant `generated-notes.json`, or validate using the same safe basename/containment helper.
Tradeoff: old custom filenames would no longer load, but none are part of the v1 schema.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Traversal generated-notes filenames are rejected or ignored safely.
- [ ] Valid generated notes still load.
- [ ] Full macOS test suite passes.
close_reason: Fixed: generated-notes load rejects manifest filenames other than generated-notes.json and has traversal regression coverage.

title: Spike: Phase 3 - Can Meetless restore signing and sandboxing without breaking local session storage?
close_reason: YES: forced signed Debug build succeeded locally, sandbox entitlements were present on the built app, and the storage code already resolves through Application Support APIs; remaining work is explicit entitlements wiring and runtime container-path verification.

title: Resolve Review P2: Route toolbar navigation through the shared screen router
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
The top toolbar picker changes screens without going through `AppModel.show(...)`, so navigation side effects only happen for some navigation paths. That makes the UI state inconsistent depending on which control the user clicks.

## What The Code Does Today
- The segmented picker binds directly to `$appModel.selectedScreen`.
- `AppModel.show(_:)` contains the logic that refreshes history and shows the empty-detail state when needed.
- Buttons inside the views call `show(...)`, but the toolbar picker does not.

## Why This Is A Problem
- The same navigation action should not behave differently based on which control triggered it.
- Today, using the picker can skip the refresh that the History screen expects and can bypass the “no selection” setup for Session Detail.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- The user records a meeting, stops, and then clicks `History` in the segmented toolbar.
- `selectedScreen` changes, but `show(.history)` never runs.
- History opens without the automatic refresh path that the rest of the app uses, so the user may see stale rows until they manually reload.

## Evidence
**File:** `MeetlessApp/App/MeetlessRootView.swift`
**Line(s):** 21-30

```swift
ToolbarItem(placement: .principal) {
    Picker("Screen", selection: $appModel.selectedScreen) {
        ...
    }
}
```

**File:** `MeetlessApp/App/AppModel.swift`
**Line(s):** 28-43

```swift
func show(_ screen: AppScreen) {
    selectedScreen = screen

    switch screen {
    case .history:
        Task { await refreshSavedSessions() }
    case .sessionDetail:
        if selectedSessionID == nil {
            sessionDetailViewModel.showNoSelection()
        }
    case .home:
        break
    }
}
```

**Why this is a problem:** navigation state changes are split between direct property writes and the real routing method.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: bind the picker through a custom setter that calls `show(...)`
**Pros:** Keeps one routing path for all navigation actions.
**Cons:** Slightly more SwiftUI binding code.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — move the side effects into `selectedScreen` observation
**Pros:** Covers all screen changes centrally.
**Cons:** Can become harder to reason about if other setters depend on ordering.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Switching screens from the toolbar picker runs the same side effects as the in-view navigation buttons.
- [ ] Entering `History` from any UI path refreshes saved sessions consistently.
- [ ] Entering `Session Detail` with no selected session still shows the empty-detail state instead of a stale previous view.

description:

title: Resolve Review P3: Test idempotent Gemini key deletion
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
The Keychain store treats deleting a missing Gemini key as success, but that behavior is not covered by a test.

## What The Code Does Today
- `deleteAPIKey` returns success for `errSecSuccess` and `errSecItemNotFound`.

## Why This Is A Problem
- Settings should allow clearing an already-missing key without an avoidable error.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- User clears the key twice, or the key is removed externally, and a future refactor starts surfacing an error.

## Evidence
File: `MeetlessApp/Services/GeminiAPIKeyStore/GeminiAPIKeyStore.swift`
Line(s): 102, 105
Snippet: `case errSecSuccess, errSecItemNotFound:`
Why this proves the issue: behavior exists but is not directly asserted.

## Proposed Fix
Recommended: add a test that calls `deleteAPIKey()` on an empty fake store and asserts no throw.
Tradeoff: small extra unit-test case.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Missing-key delete is directly tested.
- [ ] Full macOS test suite passes.
close_reason: Fixed: added missing-key delete idempotency test for Gemini API key store.

title: Resolve Review P1: Freeze the saved transcript at the user-visible Stop snapshot
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
After the user presses Stop, the app currently flushes more transcript windows and can save text that was never visible before the stop action. That conflicts with the locked Phase 1 contract.

## Current Behavior
`stopShellSession()` stops capture first, then calls `finishPendingWork()` ([MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:740](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)). `finishPendingWork()` explicitly requests flushes on every source lane and keeps scheduling additional transcript windows until buffers are empty ([MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:239](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)).

## Why It Matters
The contract says v1 does not do a final post-recording transcription pass (`D12`) and that the saved transcript snapshot must match what the operator saw during recording (`D23`). The current stop behavior can rewrite that snapshot after capture has already ended.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
The operator presses Stop while there are still buffered frames waiting to transcribe. The app spends extra time processing those windows after stop, then persists extra chunks into `transcript.json`. The reopened session now shows transcript text the operator never saw on-screen before stopping.

## Evidence
- Stop path: [MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:740](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)
- Flush behavior: [MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:239](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)
- Locked decisions: [history/native-macos-meeting-recorder/CONTEXT.md:24](./history/native-macos-meeting-recorder/CONTEXT.md), [history/native-macos-meeting-recorder/CONTEXT.md:30](./history/native-macos-meeting-recorder/CONTEXT.md)

## Smallest Credible Fix Direction
Treat the last visible `currentTranscriptChunks()` snapshot as authoritative when Stop is pressed. Do not schedule new transcript work after stop; at most allow already in-flight work to settle if that does not change the persisted contract, otherwise freeze immediately and mark leftover audio as untranslated backlog.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Pressing Stop does not create new committed transcript chunks that were not already visible.
- `transcript.json` matches the user-visible timeline at the moment of Stop.
- The stop path remains consistent with `D12` and `D23`.
close_reason: Changed Stop to persist the frozen on-screen transcript snapshot instead of flushing new post-stop windows, keeping the saved transcript aligned with the user-visible recording timeline.

title: Resolve Review P2: Remove production runtime access to snapshot-failure injection
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
A review-only fault-injection switch is still live in the normal runtime code path. Anyone who can launch the app with a custom environment can intentionally force transcript snapshot writes to fail.

## What The Code Does Today
- `SessionRepository` defines `MEETLESS_FORCE_TRANSCRIPT_SNAPSHOT_UPDATE_FAILURE` as a runtime environment key.
- `shouldForceTranscriptSnapshotWriteFailure()` reads that environment in normal builds whenever the test override is absent.
- `updateTranscriptSnapshot(...)` uses it to deliberately throw before writing `transcript.json`.

## Why This Is A Problem
- This is test-only behavior sitting in the shipping persistence path.
- It is not a remote exploit, but it does create an avoidable integrity backdoor: launched under the wrong environment, Meetless will intentionally keep saved transcript snapshots stale while appearing otherwise functional.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- A local launcher script or inherited shell environment sets `MEETLESS_FORCE_TRANSCRIPT_SNAPSHOT_UPDATE_FAILURE=1`.
- The user records a meeting and stops normally.
- Meetless intentionally throws on every snapshot update, so reopened sessions show stale transcript state even though the user never opted into a review/test mode.

## Evidence
**File:** `MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/SessionRepository.swift`
**Line(s):** 202-205, 367-383, 484-490

```swift
private static let forcedSnapshotFailureEnvironmentKey = "MEETLESS_FORCE_TRANSCRIPT_SNAPSHOT_UPDATE_FAILURE"
static var testForcedTranscriptSnapshotFailureOverride: Bool?
...
if Self.shouldForceTranscriptSnapshotWriteFailure() {
    throw CocoaError(.fileWriteUnknown)
}
...
let value = ProcessInfo.processInfo.environment[forcedSnapshotFailureEnvironmentKey]
return value == "1" || value?.lowercased() == "true"
```

**Why this is a problem:** the production repository path is still reading a test fault-injection environment variable.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: compile-gate the environment path to debug/test builds only
**Pros:** Keeps the review harness while removing the shipping runtime hook.
**Cons:** Slightly more build-configuration branching.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — remove the environment hook and keep only explicit test overrides
**Pros:** Simplest production surface.
**Cons:** Loses a convenient manual injection tool for local review runs.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Production/runtime builds do not read `MEETLESS_FORCE_TRANSCRIPT_SNAPSHOT_UPDATE_FAILURE`.
- [ ] Transcript snapshot failure injection remains available only through test-only seams or debug-only code paths.
- [ ] Existing regression coverage still proves the stale-snapshot honesty path without depending on shipping runtime environment state.

title: Resolve Review P1: Reject generated-notes overwrite
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
Meetless currently lets generated notes be saved more than once for the same session. V1 promises notes are permanent after first generation, so a second save must be rejected.

## What The Code Does Today
- `saveGeneratedNotes` loads the manifest, assigns `generatedNotesFilename`, and replaces existing `generated-notes.json` if present.

## Why This Is A Problem
- Locked decision D7 says generated notes disable future generation in v1, with no overwrite/version-history flow.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- A retry bug or UI race calls `saveGeneratedNotes` twice and silently replaces trusted notes.

## Evidence
File: `MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/SessionRepository.swift`
Line(s): 470, 500
Snippet: `let hadExistingNotes = fileManager.fileExists(atPath: notesURL.path)`
Why this proves the issue: the write path explicitly handles existing notes and does not reject them.

## Proposed Fix
Recommended: add a dedicated already-generated error and fail before staging when the manifest already points to generated notes or the notes file exists.
Tradeoff: later regeneration will need an explicit new API.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A second generated-notes save throws a specific error.
- [ ] Existing manifest and notes bytes are unchanged after the second save attempt.
- [ ] Full macOS test suite passes.
close_reason: Fixed: saveGeneratedNotes now rejects existing generated notes and tests prove prior bundle bytes are preserved.

title: Resolve Review P2: Move upload presentation metadata out of SessionRepository
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
The repository resolver currently returns storage facts mixed with display copy and provider-oriented metadata. That works for Phase 1, but it couples persistence to upload/UI concerns.

## What The Code Does Today
- `SessionAudioArtifactForUpload` includes `fileExtension`, `mimeType`, `displayName`, and `sourceDescription`.

## Why This Is A Problem
- Localization, provider MIME requirements, or future upload changes would force edits in persistence code.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- Phase 2 changes Gemini MIME handling or Phase 3 changes display wording, causing repository churn and broad test changes.

## Evidence
File: `MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/SessionRepository.swift`
Line(s): 101, 451, 753
Snippet: `mimeType: Self.mimeType(forAudioFilename: artifact.filename)`
Why this proves the issue: repository owns presentation/provider mapping, not only bundle artifact resolution.

## Proposed Fix
Recommended: in Phase 2, narrow repository output to storage facts and move MIME/display mapping to the Gemini service or view-model boundary.
Tradeoff: a later migration will update the Phase 2 consumer contract.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Repository resolver returns only bundle-owned artifact facts.
- [ ] Gemini/upload layer owns MIME/provider metadata.
- [ ] UI/view-model owns display copy.
- [ ] Tests are updated at the new boundaries.

title: Resolve Review P1: Make whisper bootstrap reproducible on a clean checkout
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
The app currently builds only if a locally generated `Vendor/whisper.cpp/build-apple/whisper.xcframework` already exists. That framework is not tracked in git, so a clean checkout cannot build Phase 1 at all.

## Current Behavior
The Xcode project references `Vendor/whisper.cpp/build-apple/whisper.xcframework` directly ([Meetless.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj:54](./Meetless.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj)). Locally, that path exists because the nested vendor repo has generated artifacts, but `git ls-files -s Vendor/whisper.cpp` shows the path is a gitlink and a clean local clone produced an empty `Vendor/whisper.cpp` directory with no `build-apple` output.

## Why It Matters
This breaks the Phase 1 build reproducibility promise around the whisper packaging boundary. A teammate, CI job, or fresh machine cannot even compile the app without manual local state that is not encoded in the repo.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
A fresh clone at `/tmp/meetless-review-yzY69Q/clean` failed with `There is no XCFramework found at ... Vendor/whisper.cpp/build-apple/whisper.xcframework` when running `xcodebuild -project Meetless.xcodeproj -scheme Meetless -configuration Debug -sdk macosx build`.

## Evidence
- Project reference: [Meetless.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj:54](./Meetless.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj)
- Local bootstrap helper: [scripts/build-whisper-xcframework.sh:1](./scripts/build-whisper-xcframework.sh)
- Approach expects bootstrap/setup to make this reproducible: [history/native-macos-meeting-recorder/approach.md:159](./history/native-macos-meeting-recorder/approach.md)

## Smallest Credible Fix Direction
Make the framework bootstrap explicit and reproducible from the repo itself. That can be a tracked setup path that initializes the vendor dependency and builds `whisper.xcframework`, or a committed/prebuilt artifact strategy, but the project must not rely on untracked local state.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Reviewer can repeat the clean-checkout build without depending on pre-existing local artifacts.

title: Resolve Review P2: Remove production generated-notes failure env hook
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
The generated-notes write failure hook can be triggered by an environment variable in normal runtime. That creates an avoidable production kill switch.

## What The Code Does Today
- `shouldForceGeneratedNotesWriteFailure` checks `MEETLESS_FORCE_GENERATED_NOTES_WRITE_FAILURE`.

## Why This Is A Problem
- A launch environment can force all generated-notes saves to fail.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- App launches with `MEETLESS_FORCE_GENERATED_NOTES_WRITE_FAILURE=1`; every notes save throws before writing.

## Evidence
File: `MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/SessionRepository.swift`
Line(s): 269, 791
Snippet: `ProcessInfo.processInfo.environment[forcedGeneratedNotesFailureEnvironmentKey]`
Why this proves the issue: non-test runtime can trigger the failure path.

## Proposed Fix
Recommended: remove the env lookup for generated notes or compile-gate it to debug/test only; keep explicit test override for tests.
Tradeoff: manual env-based fault injection is no longer available for this path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Production code path no longer reads the generated-notes failure env var.
- [ ] Tests can still exercise failure behavior through an explicit test seam.
- [ ] Full macOS test suite passes.
close_reason: Fixed: generated-notes failure path no longer reads a production environment variable; tests use explicit override seams.

title: Resolve Review P2: Finish path-safe public error logging
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
Meetless still writes raw error strings to public logs in a few failure paths, and some of those errors include absolute filesystem paths. That means the privacy hardening pass is incomplete even though directory IDs were already redacted.

## What The Code Does Today
- `WhisperBridgeError.failedToInitializeModel(path:)` builds its message from the full `url.path`.
- `WhisperSourceWorker` throws that error when model loading fails.
- `RecordingViewModel` logs `error.localizedDescription` publicly for `performToggle()` and session-begin failures.
- The permanent transcript-window failure log also writes the raw failure message publicly.

## Why This Is A Problem
- Phase 3 explicitly aimed to keep full storage paths out of public logs.
- A model-load or start-up failure can still leak `/Users/...` or container path details into exported diagnostics, which weakens the app’s local-first privacy story.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- The bundled whisper model is missing or unreadable.
- `WhisperSourceWorker` throws `Failed to initialize whisper from /Users/tester/.../ggml-tiny.en.bin.`
- `RecordingViewModel` logs that description with `privacy: .public`, so the user’s local path structure lands in a public log record.

## Evidence
**File:** `MeetlessPackages/WhisperCppBridge/Sources/WhisperCppBridge/WhisperBridge.swift`
**Line(s):** 4-17

```swift
case failedToInitializeModel(path: String)
...
case let .failedToInitializeModel(path):
    return "Failed to initialize whisper from \(path)."
```

**File:** `MeetlessPackages/WhisperCppBridge/Sources/WhisperCppBridge/WhisperSourceWorker.swift`
**Line(s):** 74-77

```swift
let context = url.path.withCString { whisper_init_from_file_with_params($0, params) }
guard let context else {
    throw WhisperBridgeError.failedToInitializeModel(path: url.path)
}
```

**File:** `MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift`
**Line(s):** 383-387, 798, 1153

```swift
logger.error("transcription window failed for ...: \(message, privacy: .public)")
...
logger.error("session bundle begin failed: \(error.localizedDescription, privacy: .public)")
...
self.logger.error("performToggle failed: \(error.localizedDescription, privacy: .public)")
```

**Why this is a problem:** public redaction currently covers session identifiers, but not path-bearing error text.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: sanitize at the error source and keep raw details private
**Pros:** Fixes the root cause once, preserves useful public logs, keeps full details available in private logs.
**Cons:** Requires touching both error construction and logging sites.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — downgrade affected error logs to `.private`
**Pros:** Fastest safety fix.
**Cons:** Public diagnostics become less descriptive and path-bearing strings may still exist internally.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No public log line emits an absolute model path, artifact path, or container path through `error.localizedDescription` or derived strings.
- [ ] Whisper/model-load failures still provide a public-safe debugging signal such as a filename, session ID, or generic failure reason.
- [ ] A regression test or log-focused assertion proves the public-safe path no longer contains `/Users/`, `Application Support`, or equivalent absolute-path content.

title: Resolve Review P2: Make compact saved-session rows fit the minimum shell width
description:
## Plain-Language Summary

The compact Saved Sessions table is slightly wider than the app canvas at the declared minimum window width. At the smallest supported size, the fixed columns and spacing can overflow or squeeze the row actions instead of cleanly fitting.

## What The Code Does Today

- The macOS window declares `minWidth: 960`.
- The shell uses a 190-point sidebar and 28-point canvas padding on both sides.
- The History table uses a 220-point minimum name column, fixed date/duration/status/action columns, and four 12-point gaps.

## Why This Is A Problem

- The Phase 3 bead acceptance criteria required compact rows to fit at default and minimum shell sizes without overlap.
- At minimum width, the content area is approximately `960 - 190 - 1 - 56 = 713` points. The table columns require about `220 + 142 + 82 + 94 + 132 + 48 = 718` points before row padding. That is enough to cause clipping or compression in the tightest supported window.

## Concrete Failure Scenario

- A user narrows Meetless to the app minimum width of 960 points.
- They open Saved Sessions.
- The table still needs a little more horizontal space than the canvas provides.
- The right-side Detail/Delete action area may clip, squeeze, or look misaligned even though this is a supported window size.

## Evidence

**File:** `MeetlessApp/App/MeetlessApp.swift`
**Line(s):** 50-51

```swift
MeetlessRootView()
    .frame(minWidth: 960, minHeight: 680)
```

**File:** `MeetlessApp/Design/MeetlessDesignTokens.swift`
**Line(s):** 20-23

```swift
static let sidebarWidth: CGFloat = 190
static let contentPadding: CGFloat = 28
```

**File:** `MeetlessApp/Features/History/HistoryView.swift`
**Line(s):** 109-120, 133-153

```swift
HStack(spacing: 12) {
    columnLabel("Name")
        .frame(minWidth: 220, maxWidth: .infinity, alignment: .leading)
    columnLabel("Date")
        .frame(width: 142, alignment: .leading)
    columnLabel("Duration")
        .frame(width: 82, alignment: .leading)
    columnLabel("Status")
        .frame(width: 94, alignment: .leading)
    columnLabel("Action")
        .frame(width: 132, alignment: .trailing)
}
```

**Why this is a problem:** These fixed widths exceed the minimum available content width by a few points, so the table is not guaranteed to fit the app’s supported minimum size.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: Trim fixed column widths or gaps
**Pros:** Small local visual fix; keeps the current compact table structure.
**Cons:** Very tight labels may need shorter action text or icon-only actions.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — Add a narrower responsive row variant
**Pros:** More robust for future smaller window sizes.
**Cons:** More view branching for a small current overflow.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Saved Sessions rows fit without clipping at the declared 960-point minimum window width.
- [ ] Detail and Delete remain accessible at minimum width.
- [ ] Existing xcodebuild test command passes.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Can Meetless use Keychain generic-password storage for Gemini keys?
description:
## Validation Spike

Question: Can Meetless use macOS Keychain generic-password APIs for the Gemini API-key seam in this target without requiring a planning redesign?

## Current Phase Risk

Phase 1 marks Keychain API-key storage as HIGH risk because it stores a user secret and must not leak into session bundles, logs, UserDefaults, or plain config.

## YES Criteria

- Local toolchain can import Security and use generic-password constants/functions.
- A validation-only generic-password add/read/update/delete path works or the failure mode is specific enough to route into implementation constraints.
- Execution can proceed with an injectable key-store protocol and focused tests.

## NO Criteria

- Keychain APIs are unavailable to the target/toolchain.
- The basic storage path requires an entitlement or runtime setup that is absent and cannot be isolated.
- Validation discovers this should return to planning before execution.
close_reason:
YES result. Local Swift toolchain imported Security and successfully performed a validation-only generic-password add/read/update/read/delete/not-found cycle using service `com.meetless.validation.gemini-key` and account `codex-validation`. The test item was deleted at the end.

Execution constraints:
- Implement a small injectable key-store protocol around SecItemAdd, SecItemCopyMatching, SecItemUpdate, and SecItemDelete.
- Keep direct Keychain calls outside SwiftUI view code.
- Do not log the key or store it in session bundles, UserDefaults, or plain config.
- Tests may use a fake store for most behavior; direct Keychain coverage can be focused and cleanup-owned.

title: Resolve Review P2: Keep History usable when one saved bundle is corrupt
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
One damaged saved-session bundle can currently blank the entire History screen. The app throws out every other valid session instead of skipping the bad bundle and continuing.

## What The Code Does Today
- `SessionRepository.listSavedSessions()` walks each session directory and decodes `session.json` inside a throwing `compactMap`.
- If any one directory has a bad manifest, the whole method throws.
- `AppModel.refreshSavedSessions()` catches that single error and `HistoryViewModel.showLoadFailure(_:)` clears the full list.

## Why This Is A Problem
- Session history is local file-backed storage, so a single damaged folder should not hide every healthy recording.
- This breaks the browse-only history contract in practice because one corrupt bundle makes the full history surface look empty or broken.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- The user has 12 saved meetings on disk.
- One `session.json` file is truncated after an interrupted write or manual filesystem cleanup.
- When they open History, Meetless throws while decoding that one directory, clears `rows`, and shows a full-screen load failure instead of the remaining 11 valid sessions.

## Evidence
**File:** `MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/SessionRepository.swift`
**Line(s):** 275-320

```swift
func listSavedSessions() throws -> [PersistedSessionSummary] {
    ...
    let sessions = try candidateURLs.compactMap { directoryURL -> PersistedSessionSummary? in
        ...
        let manifest = try loadManifest(for: session)
        return PersistedSessionSummary(...)
    }

    return sessions.sorted(by: Self.sort(lhs:rhs:))
}
```

**File:** `MeetlessApp/App/AppModel.swift`
**Line(s):** 80-88

```swift
do {
    let sessions = try await sessionRepository.listSavedSessions()
    historyViewModel.showSessions(sessions)
} catch {
    historyViewModel.showLoadFailure(error)
}
```

**File:** `MeetlessApp/Features/History/HistoryViewModel.swift`
**Line(s):** 101-105

```swift
func showLoadFailure(_ error: Error) {
    rows = []
    isLoading = false
    loadErrorMessage = "Meetless could not read the saved session bundle directory: \(error.localizedDescription)"
}
```

**Why this is a problem:** one bad bundle takes down the whole history list instead of being isolated.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: skip invalid bundles during listing
**Pros:** Preserves access to healthy sessions, keeps the UI usable, matches how local storage should degrade.
**Cons:** Requires a small reporting path if you still want to surface that some bundles were skipped.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — fail soft with partial results plus warning banner
**Pros:** Makes the hidden corruption explicit to the user.
**Cons:** Slightly more UI work than a pure repository-side skip.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A corrupt or undecodable `session.json` in one session directory does not prevent healthy sessions from appearing in History.
- [ ] Meetless either skips bad bundles silently with telemetry or surfaces a partial-results warning without clearing the whole list.
- [ ] A regression test covers at least one damaged-manifest directory alongside a healthy session directory.

title: Resolve Review P1: Make Stop finalize bounded and fail-safe
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
The current stop path can fail to finish a recording cleanly. Two separate failure modes stack together: repeated transcription errors can retry forever, and an `SCStream.stopCapture()` error can abort cleanup before the session is finalized.

## Current Behavior
Inside `TranscriptCoordinator`, a failed window restores the same frame range and immediately reschedules it with no retry budget ([MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:334](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift), [MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:346](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)). `finishPendingWork()` then waits until outstanding work disappears ([MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:239](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)). Separately, `ScreenCaptureSession.stop()` throws straight through `stream.stopCapture()` before the normal finalize/reset path runs ([MeetlessApp/Services/Capture/ScreenCaptureSession.swift:150](./MeetlessApp/Services/Capture/ScreenCaptureSession.swift), [MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:740](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)).

## Why It Matters
Phase 1 is supposed to leave behind a saved session bundle even when things go wrong. Right now a bad transcription window or stop error can keep Stop spinning forever or short-circuit the incomplete-session recovery path.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
If the bundled model is missing or corrupt, every transcription window can fail. The lane keeps retrying the same window, `finishPendingWork()` never returns, and pressing Stop never completes. If ScreenCaptureKit also throws during `stopCapture()`, the coordinator exits before best-effort finalization can mark the session incomplete and clean up state.

## Evidence
- Infinite retry loop: [MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:239](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift), [MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:334](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift), [MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:346](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)
- Throwing stop path: [MeetlessApp/Services/Capture/ScreenCaptureSession.swift:150](./MeetlessApp/Services/Capture/ScreenCaptureSession.swift), [MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift:740](./MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift)

## Smallest Credible Fix Direction
Make Stop best-effort and bounded. Add retry budgeting or a terminal degraded state for failed transcript windows, and ensure `ScreenCaptureSession.stop()` always runs cleanup/finalize logic with `defer`-style guarantees even when `stopCapture()` reports an error.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Pressing Stop always returns within a bounded time.
- Transcription failures cannot loop forever on the same frame window.
- A `stopCapture()` failure still leaves the session finalized as incomplete and clears in-memory recording state.
close_reason: Made Stop bounded and best-effort by freezing transcript ingestion, capping per-window retries, dropping permanently failing windows, and allowing ScreenCaptureSession.stop() cleanup to continue after stopCapture() errors.

- No underlying saved-session metadata is removed from models or persisted artifacts.

## Verification Criteria

- Run `xcodebuild test -project Meetless.xcodeproj -scheme Meetless -destination 'platform=macOS'`; it passes, or exact environment-only failure is recorded.
- Code inspection confirms recording/capture/whisper/session repository files were not modified.
- Code inspection confirms no primary source-lane cards or `source.rawValue` badges remain in History/Detail primary UI.
- Manual/preview visual smoke covers completed, incomplete, warning, and empty transcript states where feasible.

title: Resolve Review P1: Validate manifest artifact paths before upload
description:
## Plain-Language Summary
Meetless trusts audio filenames from `session.json`. A tampered saved bundle could point the future Gemini upload flow at a file outside the session folder.

## What The Code Does Today
- `resolveAudioArtifactsForUpload` appends the manifest filename directly to the session directory and returns the file URL if it exists.

## Why This Is A Problem
- The user may confirm uploading meeting audio while the resolver returns an unrelated local file. This is a data-exposure risk.

## Concrete Failure Scenario
- Edit `session.json` so an audio artifact filename is `../../../../Documents/private.txt`; if the file exists, the resolver can return it for upload.

## Evidence
File: `MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/SessionRepository.swift`
Line(s): 433, 442, 451
Snippet: `session.directoryURL.appendingPathComponent(artifact.filename, isDirectory: false)`
Why this proves the issue: manifest-controlled filename is not validated as a safe in-bundle audio basename.

## Proposed Fix
Recommended: reject non-basename filenames, enforce standardized URL containment under the session directory, and allow only supported audio extensions.
Tradeoff: corrupted/hand-edited bundles fail explicitly instead of attempting recovery.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Traversal filenames are rejected before file existence/upload resolution.
- [ ] Non-audio extensions are rejected.
- [ ] Valid M4A/WAV artifacts still resolve.
- [ ] Full macOS test suite passes.
close_reason: Fixed: audio artifact resolver now rejects unsafe filenames, traversal paths, and unsupported extensions with regression tests.

title: Phase 1 / Story 3: Persist session bundles and incomplete-session recovery state
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - First Live Recording Loop
What Changes: A recording no longer disappears when it ends or fails; it leaves behind a concrete local session bundle that later product phases can read.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 history, detail, delete, and incomplete-session presentation work.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Live transcript and session save
What Happens: Persist session metadata, transcript snapshot, and per-source audio artifacts on stop or interruption so unfinished sessions are incomplete rather than lost.
Contributes To: The phase exit state requiring a saved bundle and incomplete-session survivability.
Unlocks: Saved-session UX built on real local artifacts.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Store each session as a self-contained bundle under Application Support with session.json, transcript.json, meeting.wav, and me.wav, and treat the live transcript timeline as the persisted snapshot for D23.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior learnings for this domain.

## File Scope

- MeetlessApp/Services/SessionRepository/*
- MeetlessApp/Services/Transcription/* where snapshot hooks are required
- MeetlessApp/Features/Recording/* for stop/failure finalization hooks
- local fixture/sample session paths if needed for validation

## Task

- Define the on-disk session bundle contract for Phase 1.
- Persist session metadata, transcript snapshot, and per-source audio artifacts.
- Persist enough metadata early in the session lifecycle that interrupted recordings can still reopen as incomplete.
- Snapshot the committed transcript timeline incrementally during recording or immediately after committed-chunk updates, not only at graceful Stop time.
- Finalize completed sessions on Stop and preserve partial sessions on interruption.
- Expose enough status and metadata that Phase 2 can read sessions without redefining the schema.
- Honor `D7`, `D11`, and `D23` explicitly by storing a timestamp-based default title, treating per-source raw audio as the durable source of record, and saving the exact live transcript snapshot without a later rewrite.

## Verification Criteria

- Stopping a session writes the expected local bundle structure.
- Session metadata includes a timestamp-based default title suitable for Phase 2 history rows.
- Interrupted sessions still leave behind inspectable incomplete metadata and surviving artifacts.
- The saved transcript snapshot matches the committed live transcript timeline from the session.
- The saved audio artifacts remain the durable source of record for the session.
- The incremental snapshot strategy means interruption does not require a graceful Stop to preserve transcript state.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Stopping a session writes the expected local bundle structure.
- Session metadata includes a timestamp-based default title.
- Interrupted sessions still leave behind incomplete metadata and surviving artifacts.
- The saved transcript snapshot matches the committed live timeline from the session.
- The saved audio artifacts remain the durable source of record.
- Transcript state survives interruption without depending on graceful Stop.

title: Resolve Review P2: Sanitize standalone Me lane wording in saved detail notices
description:
## Plain-Language Summary

Saved detail notices still have one path where user-facing text can say `Me lane ...`. The design contract allows internal source metadata, but the primary UI should not expose `Meeting` / `Me` source lanes.

## What The Code Does Today

- `SessionDetailViewModel.compactNoticeItem(for:)` renders source notice messages after passing them through `sanitizedSourceText`.
- `sanitizedSourceText` replaces `Meeting source`, `Me source`, `Meeting`, `Me was`, and `Me is`, but it does not replace standalone strings such as `Me lane stayed healthy through stop.`

## Why This Is A Problem

- Phase 3 explicitly requires source-health/degraded state to stay visible without primary `Meeting` / `Me` source-lane cards or badges.
- Decision D1/D15 still require source truth internally, but the approved UI direction says that truth should be summarized as compact input health in the primary UI.

## Concrete Failure Scenario

- A saved session contains a source status with `source: .me`, `state: .monitoring`, and detail text `Me lane reported a recoverable capture warning.`
- `SavedSessionNoticeFactory` creates a source notice whose message is that detail text.
- Session Detail renders the notice rail as `Input health` plus `Me lane reported a recoverable capture warning.`
- The primary saved detail UI again exposes the `Me` lane wording the revamp was meant to remove.

## Evidence

**File:** `MeetlessApp/Features/SessionDetail/SessionDetailView.swift`
**Line(s):** 217-223, 262-269

```swift
if notice.id.hasPrefix("source-") {
    return NoticeItem(
        id: notice.id,
        severity: notice.severity,
        title: "Input health",
        message: sanitizedSourceText(notice.message)
    )
}

private static func sanitizedSourceText(_ text: String) -> String {
    text
        .replacingOccurrences(of: "Meeting source", with: "Input")
        .replacingOccurrences(of: "Me source", with: "Input")
        .replacingOccurrences(of: "Meeting", with: "Input")
        .replacingOccurrences(of: "Me was", with: "Input was")
        .replacingOccurrences(of: "Me is", with: "Input is")
}
```

**Why this is a problem:** The helper misses common persisted strings like `Me lane ...`, so the compact rail can still expose a source lane label.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A — Recommended: Replace lane/source wording more broadly
**Pros:** Small, local UI-display fix; preserves persisted data untouched.
**Cons:** String replacement is still a display shim.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B — Build explicit display messages from state
**Pros:** Avoids relying on persisted detail text wording.
**Cons:** Slightly less specific for source notices unless detail is summarized elsewhere.
**Effort:** Small to Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Source notice messages shown in History/Detail never include primary `Meeting` or `Me` lane/source wording.
- [ ] Persisted source metadata remains unchanged.
- [ ] Existing xcodebuild test command passes.
